data.title: Trump Media Stock Takes a Hit as Tariff Storm Hits and Stock Sale Looms
data.description:
Trump Media & Technology Group Corp. (DJT) saw its stock tumble by 4% to $17.93 on April 3, 2025, following President Trump's latest global tariff announcement. This marks a significant blow for the stock, which has continued its downward spiral since the start of the week.

DJT Stock Sale Looms

But it wasn't just the tariffs that knocked Trump Media. In a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), the company revealed that a trust controlled by Trump could sell up to $2.15 billion worth of stock. While Trump Media clarified there are no immediate plans to follow through, the mere potential of such a massive sale sent investors scrambling. The worry is simple: increased stock supply could dilute shares, further eroding the stock's already shaky standing.

DJT Stock Shifts from Election Hope to Investor Letdown

Trump Media's stock has always been subject to the whims of political tides. The stock surged to $51 leading up to the November 2024 election, fueled by optimism over Trump's political future. However, once the election results were confirmed, the stock nosedived as investor enthusiasm quickly evaporated. DJT stock's post-election collapse mirrored the market's shifting confidence in Trump's comeback prospects.

Trump's Tariffs Shake More Than Just the Auto Industry

And the pain didn't stop there. On the same day Trump's stock took a hit, the announcement of sweeping tariffs added more fuel to the fire. A 10% baseline tariff on imports and additional tariffs on major trading partners like China and the European Union further unsettled markets. The tariffs triggered a ripple effect, with futures tracking the S&P 500 dropping 3.7% in response.

The auto industry, already facing supply chain challenges, felt the immediate impact of these tariffs. The additional costs on imported car parts and vehicles are expected to raise prices for both domestic and foreign-made cars. This has sparked concerns that higher prices could dampen consumer demand, especially for imported models, which make up nearly half of the U.S. car market. The ripple effects are already being felt by automakers like General Motors (GM) and Volkswagen (VWAGY), who are bracing for potential losses as these tariffs tighten their margins.

DJT Stock Dip Reflects Uncertain Future

Trump Media's future remains uncertain, especially with the potential stock sale hovering like a storm cloud. As Business Insider explained, while no immediate actions are planned, the mere threat of these stock sales has left a lingering sense of unease. Naturally, DJT stock has plunged by 3.1% over the past 5 days. At the time of writing, DJT stock is trading at $17.93.

data.author: Annika Masrani

data.title: Microsoft Hits Pause on Data Centers, Raising AI Demand Questions and Stock Market Concerns
data.description:
According to Bloomberg's sources, Microsoft (MSFT) is halting some of its data center projects worldwide, which is raising eyebrows across the tech industry.

The company, a major player in cloud services and artificial intelligence thanks to its partnership with OpenAI, has reportedly paused or backed out of projects in places like Indonesia, the UK, Australia, Illinois, North Dakota, and Wisconsin. These data centers are the backbone of AI tools and cloud computing, so any shift in plans gets people talking.

Why Is Microsoft Backtracking?

What's going on here? Well, it's not exactly clear-cut. Some think Microsoft is just running into normal construction delays. Things like power supply problems, labor shortages, or the rising cost of building materials. Others see something bigger: a sign that the red-hot demand for AI services may be cooling off a bit, or at least not growing fast enough to justify building a small city's worth of server farms.

Moreover, Microsoft isn't giving up on many details. A company spokesperson said they plan data center needs "years in advance" and that the recent changes show how "flexible" MSFT's strategy is. In other words, they're adjusting as they go.

Still, there are hints that more is going on. Microsoft has walked away from talks to lease space at a major data center project near London and backed out of a similar deal near Chicago. It also paused construction at a site in Wisconsin where nearly $40 million had already been spent on concrete alone and delayed progress on a campus near Jakarta. Meanwhile, Microsoft's cloud partner CoreWeave (CRWV) said the company recently passed on buying more server capacity, though it found other buyers for the space.

Slowing and Reevaluating

Some experts think the company is rethinking its big spending plans. Microsoft had said it would pour $80 billion into data centers this fiscal year, but going forward, the focus may shift from building brand-new locations to upgrading existing ones.

Part of this may be tied to OpenAI. Microsoft's longtime partner is now teaming up with Oracle (ORCL) and SoftBank (SFTBY) in a separate venture ("Stargate") that could build its own massive AI infrastructure, possibly reducing the need for Microsoft's support.

Add it all up, and the big takeaway is that Microsoft isn't pulling out of the AI race but is slowing down to rethink the map. Maybe the future of AI doesn't need quite as many mega data centers as once thought. Perhaps efficiency is the new arms race. And with tech stocks already feeling the pressure, everyone's watching to see who slows down next.

What Is MSFT Stock Price Target?

Microsoft is considered a Strong Buy based on 35 ratings. Its average price target is $510.03, implying a 33.47% upside potential.

See more MSFT analyst ratings

data.title: Your Next Car Could Cost $10,000 More due to Trump's Automaker Tariffs
data.description:
President Trump's new 25% tariffs on cars and car parts are about to make your next new car significantly more expensive. Experts warn that these tariffs could raise prices by as much as $10,000, putting a hefty dent in your wallet if you’re planning to buy a vehicle anytime soon.

Automakers Respond to Tariffs with Higher Costs

Volkswagen (VWAGY) is already feeling the heat, adding an "import fee" to its vehicles sold in the U.S., according to The Wall Street Journal. The company's memo to dealers explains that this will force them to either raise car prices or risk losing money. This move marks the beginning of a broader trend, as car manufacturers adjust to the new tariff landscape.

Import fees and fewer dealer incentives are expected to push the cost of a new car higher. Bank of America Securities analyst John Murphy predicts that the industry could lose 3 million car sales annually as higher prices deter consumers. The average price of a new car in the U.S. is already approaching $50,000, and these tariff-induced price hikes will make the cost of ownership even steeper.

Tariffs Impact Both Imports and Domestic Manufacturers

While imported cars will bear the brunt of the tariff increases, domestic manufacturers aren't immune. Car parts from overseas will now be more expensive, making vehicles produced in the U.S. more costly as well. Companies like General Motors (GM) may need to raise prices on their Mexican-made models to stay competitive, potentially leading to an across-the-board price bump.

Tariffs Drive Higher Car Prices and Shrink Dealer Incentives

For now, car buyers will likely see little immediate change, as manufacturers and dealers work through their existing inventory. However, experts predict that these price hikes will unfold gradually over the next 6 to 12 months. Fewer incentives and higher base prices will push the cost of a new car even further out of reach for many Americans.

The auto industry is bracing for a shift, and with it, potential changes in consumer demand. Whether higher prices will slow down car sales or if the industry can adjust to the new normal is still uncertain.

Investors can track how auto stocks react to these developments by comparing them on the TipRanks Stocks Comparison tool. Click on the image below to find out more.

data.title: Quantum Computing News: Tariffs Put Small Quantum Stocks Under Pressure
data.description:
On April 2, 2025, the U.S. government imposed new tariffs on imported goods, with rates ranging from 10% on British goods to 34% on Chinese products. This move, designed to promote domestic production and reduce foreign dependence, may have unintended consequences for one of the country’s most promising but fragile sectors: quantum computing.

Importing Materials from All Around the Globe

At first glance, these tariffs might seem like just another bump in the road of international trade. But quantum startups don't live in a world of commodity parts or generic supplies. Their systems rely on a precise, global web of vendors to function. For example, IonQ's (IONQ) trapped-ion computers require ultra-pure laser systems from Germany. Superconducting qubit arrays depend on dilution refrigerators sourced from the UK. Photonic sensors come from Canada, and quantum memory modules often trace back to labs in Japan or South Korea.

This is not the kind of hardware you can swap out with a domestic replacement overnight. In most cases, there is no backup source.

Big Players Are Less Affected

As news of the tariffs broke, quantum computing stocks began to react. IonQ, Rigetti Computing (RGTI), and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) — three of the sector's best-known public players — all saw modest but immediate declines. These companies are not yet profitable, and their business models depend on continued research, rapid iteration, and access to high-performance parts. Add a sudden 20% cost rise on core components, timelines start to stretch, prototypes get shelved, and quarterly burn rates become harder to justify. The market knows this. That's why it moved.

By contrast, the industry's larger players, like IBM (IBM), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), appear largely unaffected. These firms have the capital and scale to absorb higher component prices. Sometimes, they manufacture key parts in-house or have long-term stockpiles. They can reroute supply chains or buy their way around delays if needed. In a worst-case scenario, they simply ride out the storm. However, that resilience is not available to the smaller players.

Innovation, Reputation, and Logistic Hurdles

And that's where the risk lies. Quantum innovation in the U.S. isn't driven solely by tech titans. Some of the most creative approaches — from hybrid quantum-classical models to experimental chip layouts — come from small teams operating on tight grants, venture backing, or one government contract at a time. These are the companies that move fast, take risks, and try things no boardroom would approve. They are the ones with the most to lose.

Stock pressure is only the surface of the challenge. For these firms, tariffs bring a very specific kind of threat. Timelines are already long in quantum R&D but are now getting even longer. Cost models get torn up. Delays ripple through investor calls, earnings estimates, and partner agreements. A six-month shipping delay on a laser component can push back a major milestone by a year. Time and trust are your only currencies when you’re a small-cap stock.

There's also the reputational risk. In a fast-moving field like quantum, being seen as behind can be as damaging as actually being behind. If overseas competitors continue to build at pace while U.S. startups stall, the global narrative starts to shift.

Still, not all is lost. Strategic responses are possible — and may, in fact, become necessary.

Possible Solutions

One potential move is reshoring key elements of the supply chain, not entirely, but enough to insulate against shocks. That will require time, partnerships, and likely additional government support beyond the current scope of the CHIPS Act. In the interim, smaller firms can deepen ties with less tariff-affected regions, such as South Korea and India, for specialized equipment. Some may shift limited manufacturing abroad to avoid import fees altogether, though this poses its own logistical and political risks.

Another path is tighter collaboration. In the past, these startups have prided themselves on differentiation; different qubit models, different visions for scaling, and different views on error correction. They may need to work together, sharing parts of the supply chain or co-investing in fabrication access to keep their programs on track.

For now, though, the weight of these new tariffs falls most heavily on the smallest shoulders. And that's a concern not just for shareholders but for the future of American innovation. If the U.S. is serious about leading in quantum computing, it cannot afford to let the most agile players fall behind or, worse, fall apart.

A technological revolution isn't won by the biggest firms alone. It takes the entire field: the giants, yes, but also the bold and the brave, the ones still building their first breakthrough.

Using Tipranks' comparison tool helps one adopt a broader perspective on each stock and different industries. Here, we can examine how the quantum stocks discussed compare in various parameters, such as analysts' sentiments, price targets, smart scores, and other key factors.

data.title: Trump's "National Emergency" Tariffs Crush Crypto as $150B Evaporates
data.description:
Trump's "national emergency" declaration lit a fire under global markets — then stomped it out. Crypto spiked, crashed, and bled $150 billion after tariffs hit like a freight train.

Tariffs Jolt Bitcoin after Trump's Emergency Declaration

There was no slow build. In a Rose Garden speech, Donald Trump stunned markets by declaring a "national emergency" and launching a sweeping set of tariffs. "We're charging countries approximately half of what they are and have been charging us," he said — and with that, the U.S. slapped 10% tariffs on imports from 180 countries. China got the harshest blow, hit with a 54% total levy.

Crypto didn't know what to do. Bitcoin jumped to $88,500 — then tanked back to $82,876. Ethereum nosedived over 6% to $1,797. The total crypto market lost $150 billion in a flash, according to CoinGecko.

Market Whiplash Turns to Panic as Details Sink In

At first, traders thought it might be bullish. But the optimism didn't last. Rachael Lucas from BTC Markets said the move was "uncertainty relief" — just long enough for big players to cash out before the selloff. "Smaller investors hesitated," she added, as volumes jumped 46% on her exchange.

The Crypto Fear & Greed Index crashed to 25 — deep into extreme fear. Still, some see a silver lining. David Hernandez at 21Shares told Cointelegraph the shock could actually help long term: "The announcement provided much-needed clarity… institutional investors may see an opportunity."

China Hints at Retaliation while Recession Bets Surge

But clarity only goes so far. Early Thursday, China warned of retaliation. The yuan sank to a seven-week low. Robin Brooks from the IIF said on X that if China devalues its currency, "that sets off a global risk-off that hits EMs first and then… spills back to the U.S."

Markets already smell trouble. On Polymarket, recession odds for 2025 spiked past 50%. Kalshi put them at 54%. S&P 500 futures dropped 3%, and Bitcoin hovered near a bearish technical "death cross."

Some Expect Fed Cuts if Tariffs Bite Too Hard

UBS doesn't expect a full recession — just slower growth. But others think the Fed will blink. Joseph Wang at fedguy.com argued, "Big tariffs are very dovish," adding that the fallout could force the Fed to slash rates again.

For now, Trump's "national emergency" has crypto bruised and confused. The real panic might only start if Beijing strikes back. As a result, it's important for investors to keep an eye on how their favorite cryptocurrencies respond to the latest developments on the TipRanks Cryptocurrency Center. Click on the image below to find out more.

data.title: Apple Stock (AAPL) Falls as Citi Warns of a 9% Gross Margin Impact from Reciprocal Tariff on China
data.description:
The reciprocal tariff of 34% on China announced by U.S. President Donald Trump on the "Liberation Day" could impact iPhone maker Apple's gross margin by 9%, warned Citi. The 34% tariff is on top of the existing 20% tariffs on China. Citi explained that its estimate reflects a scenario where Apple fails to secure an exemption and does not pass through the higher costs to the customers. AAPL stock was down about 8% in Thursday's pre-market trading on concerns about the impact of the tariff wars.

Citi Warns of the Adverse Impact of Tariffs on Apple's Margins

Citi analyst Atif Malik reiterated a Buy rating on Apple stock with a price target of $275 but highlighted the adverse impact of tariffs on the tech giant's gross margin, given that, as per Citi's estimates, 90% of the company's manufacturing is done in China.

The tariffs announced on the "Liberation Day" will become effective on April 9. The 5-star analyst awaits further details from Apple and the Trump administration to have a better understanding of how the tariffs will be implemented.

It is worth noting that Apple also has supply chain exposure to India, which was hit with a 26% tariff. Citi estimates the tariffs imposed on India to have a 0.5% impact on Apple's gross margin.

Jefferies Weighs in on Apple's Tariff Headwinds

Meanwhile, Jefferies analyst Edison Lee reiterated a Sell rating on Apple stock with a price target of $202.33. Lee stated, "We like the Apple supply chain more than AAPL," while adding that his base case continues to assume that the company will be exempted from China tariffs.

However, in the worst-case scenario, Lee noted that 37 million iPhones made in China to be imported into the U.S. will face 54% tariffs, and the company will absorb 100% of the tariff impact to ensure that sales volume is not adversely impacted. In such a case, the analyst estimates Apple's FY25 net profit to reduce by roughly 14%.

Lee contended that even if Apple is exempted from the current tariffs, the company will need to ramp up efforts to diversify its supply chain and pay its suppliers better. The analyst added that given this backdrop, he likes selected Apple supply chain players better than the company itself. Specifically, Lee likes LY iTech for its capacity in India and Luxshare for its capacity in Vietnam.

Is AAPL Stock a Buy, Hold, or Sell?

Amid competitive pressures faced by iPhone and Apple's other products and intense tariff woes, Wall Street is cautiously optimistic on Apple stock. The average AAPL stock price target of $251.21 implies 12.2% upside potential. AAPL stock has declined 11% so far this year.  

See more AAPL analyst ratings

data.author: Sirisha Bhogaraju

data.title: Trump's "Made In America" Tariffs Hammer China, Vietnam, and Taiwan with Shocking New Rates
data.description:
Trump's "Liberation Day" just flipped the script on international trade. In his most aggressive tariff push yet, the president revealed a slate of country-specific duties aimed at punishing foreign exporters and reviving a "Made in America" manufacturing base. It's a dramatic pivot from the global order the U.S. helped build—and it's already rattling major economies.

Vietnam Gets Blindsided Despite U.S. Business Ties

The biggest surprise? Vietnam. Once seen as the safe haven for companies fleeing Chinese tariffs, Vietnam now faces a 46% levy on goods shipped to the U.S., as outlined in the full tariff chart presented by Trump. That's even higher than China's rate.

According to Bloomberg, Vietnamese officials said they were blindsided by the move and have already launched an emergency task force in response. The timing is brutal. Major U.S. brands like Apple (AAPL), Nike (NKE), and Lululemon (LULU) had already poured money into Vietnam to dodge China-related political risks. Now, they're facing the same fire they tried to avoid.

Those companies were down in premarket trading on the news.

Taiwan Calls Tariffs “Highly Unreasonable”

Taiwan was hit with a 32% tariff. Semiconductors—its core export—were excluded for now, but the broader message didn't go unnoticed. In a statement shared directly with Focus Taiwan, government officials called the tariffs "highly unreasonable" and misaligned with the strength of U.S.–Taiwan relations. They're preparing diplomatic talks in hopes of a reversal.

China Slams “Unilateral Bullying” as Retaliation Looms

China was slapped with a 34% tariff. The Chinese Commerce Ministry said the tariffs "severely infringe legitimate rights" and represent "a typical act of unilateral bullying." While Beijing didn't specify how it would respond, officials made clear that retaliation is coming.

EU and UK Responses Reveal a Divided West

The European Union, facing a 20% tariff, issued a mixed message. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said the EU "is prepared to respond," but also offered to work with the U.S. to modernize global trade rules. She warned the tariffs could "massively" harm the world economy.

The U.K. walked away with the lightest blow: a 10% tariff. Still, Business Secretary Jonathan Reynolds said the government was "disappointed" and would push for a broader deal to protect future trade. "We're in a better position than most," he said in public remarks, "but we're not out of the woods."

While the U.S. calls these "reciprocal" tariffs, they mark a clear break from the free-trade system that's defined the post-WWII global economy. For countries like Vietnam and Taiwan—previously seen as safe havens for U.S. outsourcing—the message is loud and clear: nowhere is safe anymore.

Major U.S. Stocks Could Feel the Tariff Fallout

While global governments scramble to respond, investors are already assessing which stocks could take the brunt of these new tariffs.

Apple (AAPL) is one of the most exposed. The tech giant has ramped up production in Vietnam over the past two years to diversify away from China. Now, that strategy faces new friction. Nike (NKE) and Lululemon (LULU) are in a similar bind. Both leaned heavily on Vietnam for apparel production, and both are now staring down tariff-fueled cost increases that could squeeze margins or hit pricing.

On the chip side, Nvidia (NVDA), Qualcomm (QCOM), and AMD (AMD) all have key supplier relationships in Taiwan. While semiconductors were excluded for now, the threat of future tariffs could weigh on sentiment. As for automakers, Tesla (TSLA) and General Motors (GM) source parts from China and Europe, making them less insulated than they might appear.

Retailers like Walmart (WMT) and Target (TGT) also aren't in the clear. Much of their inventory comes from Asia, and increased import costs could either erode margins or push prices higher for U.S. shoppers.

To stay on top of how these tariffs are rippling through the market, investors can use the TipRanks Stock Comparison tool. It lets you easily evaluate key metrics—like analyst ratings, valuation, Smart Scores, and recent performance—across companies exposed to tariff risks.

data.title: Private Equity Stocks Crash as Tariffs Threaten Dealmaking for KKR and Apollo
data.description:
Private equity (PE) stocks tumbled after Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff blitz, with names like KKR (KKR), Apollo (APO), and Carlyle (CG) sinking hard as the market priced in the fallout. Investors are now questioning whether higher rates, weaker growth, and rising costs will upend the PE model—and fast.

PE Stocks Slide as Firms Brace for Higher Rates

Shares of top U.S. private equity firms dropped sharply in after-hours trading on Tuesday. KKR sank 6.4%, Apollo fell 6%, and Carlyle lost 5.4%, according to Barron's. Blackstone (BX), Blue Owl (OWL), and Ares (ARES) weren't far behind. The market's message? Tariffs could keep interest rates higher for longer—and that's bad news for leveraged buyouts.

The bigger problem? Dealmakers rely on cheap debt. But with inflation risks now climbing again, the Fed may hit pause on its expected rate cuts. Two cuts had been penciled in for 2025. That path just got murkier. Higher borrowing costs make it harder to fund deals—or exit them cleanly.

Tariffs Target Imports That Hit Portfolios Directly

This isn't just a macro scare. The specifics sting too. Trump’s new 54% tariff on Chinese goods is particularly painful for portfolio companies tied to manufacturing and consumer goods—two sectors often owned by PE firms and deeply reliant on imported components, according to The Times.

That's a problem. Rising costs can't always be passed to consumers. Supply chains don't realign overnight. And many of these businesses are already operating on thin margins. As PwC pointed out, deal IRRs shrink fast when input costs jump unexpectedly.

Private Equity Faces Hard Choices in Tougher Economy

The backdrop is messy. Inflation is sticky. Growth is wobbly. And U.S. Fiscal tightening is limiting room to maneuver. According to TD Cowen, Apollo still sees "a path to fundamental outperformance" thanks to its private credit arm, but even that comes with risks.

In this environment, PE firms may need to shift from fast exits to defensive plays. Smaller deals, longer holds, more credit. The era of easy arbitrage and cheap leverage? It's getting priced out.

Investors can use the TipRanks Stock Comparison tool to evaluate key metrics across private equity firms—like valuation, analyst ratings, and dividend yield—making it easier to spot relative strengths and weaknesses before making a move.

data.title: Global Stock Markets Fall as Trump's 'Liberation Day' Tariffs Trigger Fears of Trade Wars
data.description:
Global stock markets fell on Thursday as the tariffs announced by U.S. President Donald Trump on “Liberation Day” triggered fears of trade wars with major countries. Stocks in European and Asian markets plunged as investors are concerned that the tariffs would hurt demand and result in a potential recession.

Trump pointed out significant trade deficits and said that the new tariffs will generate $6 trillion in investments.

European and Asian Stock Markets in the Red on Tariff Woes

European stock markets opened in the red on Thursday in reaction to Trump's aggressive trade policy announcements. Notably, the Stoxx Europe 600 index was down 1.3% as of writing, with investors moving to safe-haven assets including gold, treasuries, and German Bunds. Stocks of German sportswear company Adidas (DE:ADS) (ADDYY) and shipping giant Maersk (DE:DP4H) were among the major losers. Auto stocks and stocks in the steel and aluminum space were also hit, as Trump's 25% tariffs on vehicles imported to the U.S. became effective.

Further, the UK's FTSE 100 trended lower by 1.2%. In comparison, France's CAC 40 and Germany's DAX were down 2.0% and 1.65%, respectively, as of writing.

On Wednesday, Trump announced retaliatory tariffs affecting more than 180 countries and territories, with a baseline tariff of 10% across the board. He imposed a 20% tariff on goods being imported from the EU (European Union) and a 10% tariff on goods imported from the UK.

Meanwhile, in Asia, Japanese markets led the losses, with Nikkei 225 ending about 3% lower and Topix down 3.1%. Hong Kong's Hang Seng index fell 1.52%, while China's Shanghai Composite and Shenzhen Component ended lower by 0.24% and 1.4%, respectively.

The Trump administration imposed a 24% tariff on Japan and slapped a 34% retaliatory tariff on China (on top of the existing 20% duties), contending that China charges the U.S. a 67% tariff on imports.

Overall, global trade wars are escalating with several countries threatening retaliatory measures. In fact, China’s Ministry of Commerce slammed Trump’s moves and called the decision “typical unilateral bullying practice.”

U.S. Stock Futures Plunge on Trade War Fears

The risk of global trade wars dragged down U.S. stock futures early Thursday. Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), S&P 500 (SPX), and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) were down 3.33%, 3.04%, and 2.5%, respectively, as of writing.

Stocks of companies with significant global exposure, like Apple (AAPL) and Nike (NKE), and companies dependent on imports, including retailers like Dollar Tree (DLTR), Five Below (FIVE), and Gap (GAP), were among the prominent decliners in Thursday’s pre-market trading.

data.author: Sirisha Bhogaraju

data.title: Trump Tariffs Dismantle Decades of Globalization as U.S. Demands Factories Come Home
data.description:
Trump's new tariffs aren't just about trade—they're about making “America Great Again.” On what he's dubbed "Liberation Day," the former president dropped a sweeping set of import tariffs designed to reverse decades of globalization and force companies to bring production and factories back to the U.S.

White House Pushes a Made-in-America Mandate

The policy is simple on paper: a 10% base tariff on all imports, with higher rates on top trading partners—54% for China, 46% for Vietnam, and 20% for the EU. The message is even clearer. If your goods aren't made in the U.S., they're going to cost a lot more.

Trump has long promised to overhaul global supply chains. But the scale of this new round signals a dramatic escalation. Multinational companies from automakers to tech giants now face a choice: pay the price, or shift operations closer to home.

Backlash Builds as Businesses Warn of Price Pain

Critics are already pushing back. Industry groups say the tariffs will hurt American consumers more than foreign exporters. Higher input costs, more expensive imports, and retaliatory tariffs could squeeze sectors like manufacturing, agriculture, and retail all at once.

The National Foreign Trade Council called it a "massive tax hike" on U.S. businesses. And if past trade wars are any guide, these price shocks could hit shelves fast.

The U.S. Is Turning Away from the Global Trade Model

According a Wall Street Journal report, this plan marks the most aggressive shift away from globalization by any modern U.S. administration. The paper reported that the goal is to fully reorient the economy around domestic production—signaling the end of America's decades-long support for a global, open-market system.

It's unclear whether this reshoring push will bring back jobs—or just leave consumers footing a bigger bill.

How Did the Market React to Trump’s Tariffs?

Markets weren't exactly cheering. The SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust, which tracks 500 of the largest U.S.-based companies, dropped 2.99% in pre-market trading following the tariff announcement. Sectors like industrials and tech took the biggest hit. The message from Wall Street was loud and clear—reshoring factories might sound good politically, but investors aren't buying it just yet.
